briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for April 19, 2026. Today, we will provide a comprehensive overview of significant news, including ongoing climate change response efforts both domestically and internationally, shifts in public perception regarding the climate crisis, and the climate impacts faced globally.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Climate Change Week: Spreading Carbon Neutrality Practices Nationwide
sections.content: The government-led '2026 Climate Change Week' has commenced, with a nationwide campaign underway under the slogan 'Green Transformation Completed Through Carbon Neutrality Practices' [1, 13, 23, 35, 53, 62, 69]. Notably, a 10-minute lights-out event is scheduled to take place at major landmarks across the country on Earth Day, April 22 [1, 9, 26, 62, 69]. Incheon City is extending its Climate Change Week for a total of fifteen days, ten days longer than the government's designated week, dedicating full efforts to spreading a culture of carbon neutrality practices [5, 18, 42, 48, 76, 77]. Additionally, various local governments and institutions, including the Seoul Metropolitan Office of Education, Jung-gu, Hongcheon-gun, Gyeongnam Province, Jinju City, Mokpo City, Samcheok City, Seongdong-gu, and Gyeongju City, are actively promoting citizen participation through Earth Day commemorative carbon neutrality campaigns and experiential events [4, 7, 12, 17, 24, 31, 32, 37, 38, 43, 44, 51, 54, 58, 59, 60, 61, 63, 65, 66, 67, 68, 70, 71, 74].

sections.title: ⚠️ Weakening Climate Crisis Awareness and Spreading Convenience-Oriented Mindset
sections.content: Regrettably, recent surveys indicate a weakening of public awareness regarding the severity of climate change and a growing tendency to prioritize convenience over eco-friendly actions [3, 8, 19, 20, 33, 40]. Notably, 36.2% of respondents in their 20s stated 'convenience comes before environment,' revealing a reluctance towards 'inconvenient eco-friendliness,' with overall lower eco-consciousness observed among those in their 20s and males [3, 10, 50]. International analyses also suggest that while Koreans feel anxious about climate change, their willingness to translate this into actual action is decreasing [90], underscoring the urgent need to find ways to drive tangible behavioral changes in response to the climate crisis.

sections.title: 🌱 International Cooperation and Climate Change Response Efforts
sections.content: International efforts to address climate change are also continuing. Yoon Seok-dae, CEO of K-water (Korea Water Resources Corporation), has been appointed as a special advisor to the Philippine Presidential Palace Climate Change Commission, where he will share his expertise in water management and expand cooperation on climate change response [2, 6, 11, 14, 15, 25, 27, 28, 30, 36, 39, 41, 45, 46, 47, 49]. Furthermore, Vietnam is actively pursuing energy transition to achieve its carbon neutrality goals [73], and the U.S. Department of Defense continues its efforts to prepare for climate change [88]. Climate experts have gathered to discuss the climate change issues facing Pakistan [95], and the gardener at Burghley House has highlighted the connection between estate management and climate change [87], demonstrating how various stakeholders are sharing awareness and seeking solutions to climate change issues.

sections.title: 🌱 Expansion of Renewable Energy and Greenhouse Gas Reduction Achievements
sections.content: Various regions in Korea are achieving tangible results in greenhouse gas reduction and renewable energy expansion efforts. Cheonan City reduced 110,000 tons of greenhouse gases last year, according to its carbon neutrality implementation review, with efforts in the waste and agricultural/livestock sectors being particularly effective [57, 72, 75]. Pohang City has declared its ambition to turn the climate crisis into an opportunity to establish a global carbon neutrality 'standard' [29], and Siheung City's coastal and urban wind power projects are progressing smoothly, contributing to renewable energy expansion [78]. Korea Southern Power has fully embarked on restructuring its business to reduce coal-fired power generation and transition to renewable energy under its 'Energy Transition 2050' goal [79]. H-Energy is expanding its renewable energy platform by utilizing idle rooftops for solar power generation and significantly shortening solar power plant installation times through AI agents [80, 83, 85]. Furthermore, an analysis suggesting that lowering the price of Seoul's Climate Companion Card to around 20,000 won per month could reduce private car usage by over 400,000 vehicles demonstrates the potential for carbon neutrality through smart mobility [86].

sections.title: 🔴 Major Climate Change Impacts and Warnings
sections.content: Globally, severe impacts and warnings due to climate change are resounding. In the United States, record-breaking droughts are intensifying concerns about wildfires and rising food prices [89], and these droughts are also linked to newly identified antimicrobial threats, creating complex dangers [92]. Discussions about potential scenarios when global warming reaches 2 degrees Celsius warn of future severity [91], and research indicating that native plants may perish if they fail to adapt to climate change highlights the vulnerability of ecosystems [93]. The fact that war, climate change, and AI are slated to be major agenda items at this year's UN Indigenous Peoples Forum [94] suggests that climate change is no longer confined to environmental issues but is recognized as a complex, overarching threat to society. Domestically, Korea Railroad Corporation is strengthening safety management in preparation for abnormal summer weather [21, 34], and a 'Trauma Healing Week' program for mental recovery amidst the climate crisis is being implemented [52], demonstrating preparations for the direct impacts of climate change.

briefing.closing: As we have seen in today's briefing, while various efforts and positive progress are being made to address climate change, challenges such as weakening public awareness and intensifying climate impacts are also clearly evident. Overcoming the climate crisis urgently requires sustained attention and active participation from governments, businesses, and civil society alike. This concludes today's climate briefing.
